title: Ahaziah rebuked by Elijah
summary_text: After Ahaziah falls and seeks help from Baal-zebub, Elijah confronts the king's messengers and announces that this is not a minor accident but a sign of rebellion against the LORD. Fire falls on the captains who try to seize the prophet, but mercy is shown when the third captain humbles himself. The chapter opens Kings with a sharp reminder that Israel's God speaks over every throne.
key_points: Ahaziah seeks counsel from a pagan god.
Elijah declares the king will not recover.
Fire consumes the first two hostile captains.
The third captain survives by pleading for mercy.
evidence_phrases: Is it because there is no God in Israel?

title: Elijah taken up and Elisha receives the mantle
summary_text: Elijah and Elisha travel from place to place as the younger prophet refuses to leave his master. The Jordan is parted, Elijah is taken up by a whirlwind, and Elisha receives a double portion of the prophetic spirit. The chapter marks a real transfer of ministry and confirms that the LORD's work continues after Elijah's departure.
key_points: Elijah tests Elisha's resolve at several stops.
The Jordan opens before the prophets.
Elijah is taken up in a whirlwind.
Elisha begins ministry with the same power at work.

title: Water in the wilderness and Moab's defeat
summary_text: Jehoram, Jehoshaphat, and the king of Edom march against Moab, but the campaign stalls until Elisha is consulted. God provides water without wind or rain and then grants victory over Moab. The chapter shows that military plans only succeed when the LORD provides the way.
key_points: The allied kings face water shortage in the wilderness.
Elisha promises water and victory.
The valley fills with water in an unexpected way.
Moab is driven back under divine help.
evidence_phrases: make this valley full of trenches
the water came by the way of Edom

title: Oil, bread, and life through Elisha
summary_text: Elisha helps a widow pay her debts, multiplies oil, and later provides for the Shunammite woman and her child. He also purifies a deadly pot of stew and feeds a hundred men with a small amount of bread. The chapter presents Elisha as a prophet through whom God gives life, provision, and hope.
key_points: Oil is multiplied to rescue a widow from debt.
A promised son is given to the Shunammite woman.
The boy later dies and is restored to life.
A small meal feeds many people under God's hand.
evidence_phrases: according to the word of Elisha
the deadly thing is in the pot
they ate and had some left

title: Naaman healed and Gehazi judged
summary_text: Naaman, a Syrian commander, comes for healing and learns that cleansing comes through humble obedience rather than spectacle. Elisha's instruction leads to Naaman's restoration, but Gehazi's greed brings leprosy upon himself and his descendants. The chapter contrasts faith that receives grace with a servant who turns ministry into profit.
key_points: Naaman is told to wash in the Jordan.
He is cleansed when he obeys the word of God.
Elisha refuses payment for the miracle.
Gehazi's deception leaves him with Naaman's leprosy.

title: The axe head, the army, and the blinded host
summary_text: Elisha restores a lost axe head and then protects Israel when the Aramean army comes against Dothan. God opens the eyes of Elisha's servant, blinds the enemy troops, and leads them into Samaria. The chapter reveals that unseen divine power can defend God's people more effectively than weapons.
key_points: A borrowed axe head is made to float.
The servant sees the mountain full of horses and chariots.
The enemy army is blinded and led away.
God protects his prophet and his people.
evidence_phrases: set bread and water before them

title: Four lepers discover deliverance
summary_text: Samaria is trapped by famine until four lepers stumble into the abandoned Aramean camp and find the enemy has fled. Their news reaches the king, the city is supplied, and the famine ends exactly as Elisha predicted. The chapter shows that God can turn despair into abundance through the most unlikely witnesses.
key_points: The city is starving under siege.
The lepers discover an empty camp full of supplies.
The king confirms that the Arameans have fled.
Food becomes plentiful and the prophet's word is fulfilled.
evidence_phrases: the word of the man of God

title: Shunammite restoration and Hazael foreseen
summary_text: The Shunammite woman returns and finds her land restored after Elisha's earlier warning. Elisha then meets Hazael, who will become king of Syria, and the chapter records the troubled reign of Jehoram of Judah. The story ties personal restoration to the larger political shifts that are unfolding across the region.
key_points: The Shunammite's property is returned to her.
Elisha foretells Hazael's rise.
Hazael's future brutality is exposed.
Jehoram's reign in Judah is marked by evil.
evidence_phrases: you will be king over Syria

title: Jehu anointed and the house of Ahab falls
summary_text: Jehu is anointed in secret and immediately begins the violent overthrow of Ahab's dynasty. Joram and Ahaziah are killed, and Jezebel's downfall fulfills the prophetic word spoken long before. The chapter displays God's judgment on entrenched idolatry and the end of a corrupt royal house.
key_points: Jehu is anointed to strike Ahab's house.
Joram and Ahaziah are both killed.
Jezebel is thrown down and destroyed.
The prophecy against Ahab begins to come true.

title: Jehu destroys Baal worship
summary_text: Jehu continues his purge by eliminating Ahab's sons, the Baal priests, and the remaining symbols of idolatry. He acts with ruthless zeal, but the chapter also notes that he does not fully turn from Jeroboam's sins. The result is a judgment on evil without a complete return to covenant faithfulness.
key_points: Jehu destroys the house of Ahab.
He gathers and kills the prophets of Baal.
The temple of Baal is turned into a ruin.
Jehu's zeal is real, but not complete.

title: Joash preserved and Athaliah removed
summary_text: Athaliah seizes power in Judah, but Joash is hidden and then crowned through the faithful work of Jehoiada the priest. The false queen is executed, and the covenant is renewed when the people turn back to the LORD. The chapter shows that God's promise to David survives even the darkest usurpation.
key_points: Athaliah rules by murder and fear.
Joash is hidden in the temple for protection.
Jehoiada crowns the rightful king.
Athaliah is removed and the covenant is renewed.
evidence_phrases: hid him in the house of the LORD
the king stood by the pillar

title: Joash repairs the temple
summary_text: Joash begins well by directing temple repairs and organizing generous contributions from the people. The work restores the sanctuary, though later the king is pressured into poor decisions. The chapter presents the hope of reform alongside the fragility of a king who depends too much on others.
key_points: Funds are collected to repair the temple.
The work is completed with care and honesty.
Joash's early reforms are positive.
Later political pressure reveals his weakness.
evidence_phrases: for the repair of the house

title: Jehoahaz, Jehoash, and Elisha's final sign
summary_text: Israel suffers under Aramean oppression, but the LORD still gives deliverance when kings cry out. Elisha's dying prophecy about victory is symbolized by the arrows, and his bones later bring life to a dead man. The chapter closes Elisha's ministry with a final testimony that God's power outlasts the prophet's lifetime.
key_points: Israel suffers because of repeated sin.
Elisha gives a final victory sign to Jehoash.
The prophet dies after a long ministry.
A dead man revives when touched by Elisha's bones.
evidence_phrases: the LORD gave Israel a savior
the arrow of the LORD's victory

title: Amaziah and Jeroboam II
summary_text: Amaziah wins an early victory but later makes foolish choices, and Judah is humbled by Israel's military strength. In the north, Jeroboam II expands Israel's borders, yet his success does not mean spiritual health. The chapter shows that national strength can coexist with deep covenant weakness.
key_points: Amaziah begins with some obedience.
His pride leads to defeat and humiliation.
Jeroboam II restores Israel's territory.
Political success does not equal faithfulness.
evidence_phrases: your heart has lifted you up

title: Kings fall and multiply in Israel and Judah
summary_text: Azariah, Zechariah, Shallum, Menahem, Pekahiah, and Pekah show how quickly both kingdoms can unravel when sin is entrenched. Assyria begins to press harder on the region, and internal murder becomes a way of life in the north. The chapter reads like a collapse of leadership without the healing of repentance.
key_points: Judah and Israel both drift further from the LORD.
Kings are assassinated or deposed in quick succession.
Assyria begins to dominate regional politics.
The chapter shows the cost of national unfaithfulness.

title: Ahaz turns to Assyria
summary_text: Ahaz rejects the ways of David and copies the nations around him, even altering the temple's worship to fit Assyrian pressure. He appeals to foreign power instead of trusting the LORD. The chapter shows a king who trades covenant identity for political convenience.
key_points: Ahaz copies the worship practices of the nations.
He reshapes the altar and temple setting.
Assyria becomes Judah's false security.
Covenant faith is replaced by political fear.
evidence_phrases: walked in the way of the kings of Israel
made his son pass through the fire

title: Samaria falls and Israel is exiled
summary_text: Israel's long rebellion finally ends when Assyria conquers Samaria and carries the people away. The chapter explains the exile as the result of persistent idolatry, rejected warnings, and counterfeit worship. Even as foreign settlers arrive, the land remains spiritually fractured because the people do not truly fear the LORD.
key_points: Israel persists in the sins of the northern kingdom.
Assyria captures Samaria and sends the people away.
Foreign settlers bring blended worship practices.
The chapter explains exile as covenant judgment.
evidence_phrases: removed Israel out of his sight
the king of Assyria brought people

title: Hezekiah trusts the LORD against Assyria
summary_text: Hezekiah stands apart from many earlier kings by removing idols and trusting the LORD in the face of Assyrian pressure. Sennacherib's threats expose the weakness of human confidence, but Hezekiah's reforms and faith set the stage for divine deliverance. The chapter frames Hezekiah as a reforming king who seeks God before battle and siege.
key_points: Hezekiah destroys idols and high places.
Assyria begins to pressure Judah directly.
The Rabshakeh mocks Judah's trust in God.
Hezekiah's reforms distinguish him from other kings.
evidence_phrases: Do not let Hezekiah deceive you

title: Hezekiah prays and God delivers Jerusalem
summary_text: Hezekiah spreads the enemy threats before the LORD, and Isaiah brings the promise that Jerusalem will not fall to Assyria. God answers with judgment on Sennacherib's army and then with the king's defeat at home. The chapter pairs prayer and prophecy to show that Jerusalem is preserved by divine action, not by human strength.
key_points: Hezekiah prays with the threat laid before God.
Isaiah promises that the city will be spared.
The Assyrian army is struck down.
Sennacherib later falls by violence in his own land.
evidence_phrases: he was killed with the sword

title: Hezekiah's illness and Babylonian visitors
summary_text: Hezekiah is healed after prayer, and God gives him an added sign that his life will be prolonged. Yet the arrival of Babylonian envoys reveals another danger: pride in what God has given. The chapter ends with a warning that blessing can become a test if gratitude gives way to self-display.
key_points: Hezekiah is healed after turning to God.
The shadow moves backward as a sign.
Hezekiah displays his treasures to Babylon.
Isaiah warns that the day will come when they are carried off.
evidence_phrases: all that is in my house

title: Manasseh and Amon deepen Judah's guilt
summary_text: Manasseh fills Judah with idolatry, violence, and innocent blood, and Amon follows the same path until he is assassinated. The chapter explains that Judah's coming judgment is not sudden but built on years of stubborn rebellion. Even so, the record of these kings makes clear that the LORD still speaks before final collapse.
key_points: Manasseh commits widespread evil and idolatry.
Jerusalem is stained with innocent blood.
Amon follows his father's sin and dies by conspiracy.
Judah's future judgment is being stored up.
evidence_phrases: did evil in the sight of the LORD
made his son pass through the fire
worshiped all the host of heaven

title: The book of the law found in Josiah's day
summary_text: Josiah's repair work leads to the discovery of the book of the law, and its reading cuts him to the heart. He seeks the word of the LORD through Huldah, who confirms both judgment and mercy. The chapter shows that reform begins when God's written word is heard again.
key_points: The temple repair uncovers the book of the law.
Josiah tears his clothes in repentance.
Huldah confirms coming judgment.
The king's humility sets the stage for reform.

title: Josiah's covenant reform and Passover
summary_text: Josiah leads Judah in covenant renewal by tearing down idols, cleansing the land, and restoring proper worship. He keeps a Passover unlike any since the judges, but the chapter also notes that Judah's judgment is still coming because earlier sins were so deep. The reform is sincere, yet it cannot erase the accumulated guilt of the nation.
key_points: Josiah destroys idolatrous sites and shrines.
He renews the covenant before the LORD.
The Passover is celebrated with unusual faithfulness.
Judah's doom is delayed, but not removed.
evidence_phrases: made a covenant before the LORD
there was no Passover like that

title: Judah begins its final fall
summary_text: Jehoiakim, Jehoiachin, and the first Babylonian deportation show Judah slipping into the final stage of collapse. Babylon strips away leaders, treasures, and strength while the prophets' warnings become reality. The chapter records the beginning of exile and the unraveling of David's city.
key_points: Babylon begins to control Judah's fate.
Jehoiakim rebels and suffers the consequences.
Jehoiachin is carried away into exile.
The temple treasures are removed from Jerusalem.

title: Jerusalem falls and exile is sealed
summary_text: Babylon finally breaches Jerusalem, burns the temple, and takes the remaining people into exile. Gedaliah's short administration ends in violence, and the chapter closes with Jehoiachin released from prison as a faint sign of future hope. Judgment is complete, but the story leaves a small opening for restoration.
key_points: Jerusalem is breached and the temple burned.
The people are carried off into exile.
Gedaliah's appointment ends in murder and flight.
Jehoiachin is released from prison in Babylon.
evidence_phrases: burned the house of the LORD
